reasoning: Q3 is 5.4x slower than DuckDB (540ms vs 99ms) and Q6 is 5.5x slower (110ms vs 20ms). Clear bottlenecks with concrete solutions: Robin Hood hashing for Q3 (expected 2-3x speedup), SIMD for Q6 (expected 2x speedup), sorted aggregation for Q1. All queries passing correctness. With 1 iteration remaining, we must aggressively pursue these high-impact, low-risk optimizations.
focus_areas: Replace std::unordered_map with Robin Hood hash tables in Q3 hash join (Priority 1: 2-3x expected speedup)
Switch Q1 to sorted aggregation for 4-group cardinality (Priority 4: 2x expected speedup)
Add SIMD filter evaluation to Q6 (Priority 5: 2x expected speedup)
strategy_notes: Aggressive optimization focusing on algorithm improvements. Prioritizing Q3 (biggest bottleneck at 5.4x DuckDB) with Robin Hood hashing to reduce hash lookup overhead from 8-12ns to 3-5ns over 36M probes. Q1 and Q6 are lower-hanging fruit with proven techniques (sorted agg, SIMD). Avoiding reingest-required changes (Priority 2) and high-risk NUMA changes (Priority 6) to maximize success probability in final iteration. Expected final performance: Q1 ~50ms, Q3 ~180-250ms, Q6 ~55ms = total ~285-355ms (2.1-2.7x faster than current, competitive with DuckDB ~229ms).
notes: For Robin Hood implementation: Use open addressing with backward shift deletion. Linear probing with Robin Hood heuristic (swap on probe if probe distance > incumbent distance). For Q1 sorted agg: Use direct array indexing with composite key (returnflag-'A')*2 + (linestatus=='O'?1:0) to eliminate hash overhead. For Q6 SIMD: Use AVX2 to process 4 rows at once for all filter predicates.
